coredata.dc:title: Plasmonic carbon nanohybrids for repetitive and highly localized photothermal cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Deepak S.
Kumawat, Mukesh K.
Prasad, Rajendra
Reddy, Pradeep K.
Dhanka, Mukesh
Mishra, Sumit K.
Bahadur, Rohan
Neekhra, Suditi
De, Abhijit
Srivastava, Rohit
coredata.dc:description: Integrating metallic and non-metallic platform for cancer nanomedicine is a challenging task and bringing together multi-functionality of two interfaces is a major hurdle for biomaterial design. Herein, NIR light responsive advanced hybrid plasmonic carbon nanomaterials are synthesized, and their properties toward repetitive and highly localized photothermal cancer therapy are well understood. Graphene oxide nanosheets having thickness of ∼2 nm are synthesized using modified Hummers’ method, thereafter functionalized with biodegradable NIR light responsive gold deposited plasmonic polylactic-co-glycolic acid nanoshells (AuPLGA NS, tuned at 808 nm) and NIR dye (IR780) to examine their repetitive and localized therapeutic efficacy as well resulting side effects to nearby healthy cells. It is observed that AuPLGA NS decorated graphene oxide nanosheets (GO-AuPLGA) and IR780 loaded graphene oxide nanosheets (GO-IR780) are capable in standalone complete photothermal ablation of cancer cells within 4 min. of 808 nm NIR laser irradiation and also without the aid of any anticancer drugs. However, GO-AuPLGA having the potential for repetitive photothermal treatment of a big tumor, ablate the cancer cells in highly localized fashion, without having side effects on neighboring healthy cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Plasmonic nanomaterials
Photothermal therapy
NIR dyes
Drug leaching

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Poly (DL-lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoparticles (PLGA NPs)
Preparation of gold deposited plasmonic PLGA nanoshells (AuPLGA NS)
Synthesis of AuPLGA NS decorated GO sheets (GO-AuPLGA)
Synthesis of IR780 loaded GO sheets (GO-IR780)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are thankful to PURAC, Netherlands for providing PLGA as gift sample. Also, thankful to IIT Bombay Healthcare , SAIF and IRCC for providing financial support and instrument facility. We also thank Mr. Ramkrishn Gupta from NanoBios Lab for fruitful discussion.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustrating the synthesis and comparative localized therapeutic efficacy of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780.

AFM, Micrographs, FTIR, Raman and XPS of GO. a) AFM image of GO and its section analysis showing the thickness of GO. b) FEG-SEM image of unexfoliated GO. c) FEG-TEM image of exfoliated GO sheets. d) The diffraction pattern of GO. e) FTIR spectrum of GO. f) Raman spectrum of GO showing the presence of D band and G band. g, h & i) Survey spectrum of GO (g), high-resolution deconvoluted spectra of C1s (h), and O1s (i).

Characterization of GO-AuPLGA. a, b) FEG-TEM image of AuPLGA NS (a), zoomed image (b), EDX analysis of AuPLGA NS (b-inset). c, f) FEG-TEM image of GO-PEI and its diffraction pattern (e). d, e, g) AuPLGA NS decorated over GO sheets, zoomed image and the diffraction pattern (f). h) UV–vis-NIR absorbance spectra of GO, AuPLGA NS, and GO-AuPLGA.

EDX line scan and drug loading capacity of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780. a, b) EDX line scan of GO-AuPLGA showing the profile of different elements; red triangles in image ‘a’ indicate the graphene oxide while green indicate AuPLGA NS. c, f) FEG-TEM image of GO-IR780 and its diffraction pattern (f). d, e) UV–vis-NIR spectra of GO and GO-IR780 at different concentrations, and the loading relation of IR-780 (e). g) UV–vis-NIR absorbance spectra of GO, IR780, and GO-IR780 at maximum loading efficiency. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Localized photothermal ability of graphene nanohybrids. a) Percentage biocompatibility of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA. b) Microscopic image of L929 cells in negative control, NC (left) and after GO-AuPLGA (right) treatment; scale bar 250 μm. c) Phototransduction potential of GO-IR780, GO-AuPLGA NS, GO, IR780, and water. d) Comparative therapeutic efficacy of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA at sub-therapeutic, therapeutic and double the therapeutic dosage.
Qualitative and comparative therapeutic efficacy of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA for photothermal cancer therapy in MCF 7 (a) and MDA-MB-231 (b); inset- zoomed image; scale bar 250 μm.

head.ce:title: Plasmonic carbon nanohybrids for repetitive and highly localized photothermal cancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Molecular Functional Imaging Lab, Advanced Centre for Treatment, Research and Education in Cancer (ACTREC), Tata Memorial Centre, Kharghar, Navi Mumbai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Centre for Treatment, Research and Education in Cancer (ACTREC)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, Powai, Mumbai, 400076, India.
Corresponding author at: Molecular Functional Imaging Laboratory, ACTREC, Tata Memorial Centre, Kharghar, Navi Mumbai, 410210, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Plasmonic graphene sheets for delivering the repetitive and highly localized photothermal cancer therapy.

Integrating metallic and non-metallic platform for cancer nanomedicine is a challenging task and bringing together multi-functionality of two interfaces is a major hurdle for biomaterial design. Herein, NIR light responsive advanced hybrid plasmonic carbon nanomaterials are synthesized, and their properties toward repetitive and highly localized photothermal cancer therapy are well understood. Graphene oxide nanosheets having thickness of ∼2 nm are synthesized using modified Hummers’ method, thereafter functionalized with biodegradable NIR light responsive gold deposited plasmonic polylactic-co-glycolic acid nanoshells (AuPLGA NS, tuned at 808 nm) and NIR dye (IR780) to examine their repetitive and localized therapeutic efficacy as well resulting side effects to nearby healthy cells. It is observed that AuPLGA NS decorated graphene oxide nanosheets (GO-AuPLGA) and IR780 loaded graphene oxide nanosheets (GO-IR780) are capable in standalone complete photothermal ablation of cancer cells within 4 min. of 808 nm NIR laser irradiation and also without the aid of any anticancer drugs. However, GO-AuPLGA having the potential for repetitive photothermal treatment of a big tumor, ablate the cancer cells in highly localized fashion, without having side effects on neighboring healthy cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Today, radiotherapy, chemotherapy and surgical removal are mainly used for the treatment of different types of malignancies. However, all of them meet with several shortcomings and side effects like mutations in radiotherapy, drug leaching as well as non-specific distribution to healthy tissue in chemotherapy, and the chance of relapse and acute tissue damage in surgery. Further, cancer-related fatigue (CRF) and anemia are the most common side effects related to aforementioned therapies [1,2].

To overcome the limitations of conventional therapies, hyperthermia emerged as a promising and efficient therapeutic alternative for the treatment of cancer, exploiting the higher susceptibility of cancer cells towards heat [3]. However, non-specific heating affects the nearby healthy tissues of the body. Therefore, the localized hyperthermia modalities like magnetic hyperthermia and photothermal therapies (PTT) are the emerging need for the treatment of solid tumors. Since few decades, near-infrared (NIR) light directed PTT has gained much interest in nanomedicine, and is being considered as an advanced and emerging therapeutic approach to cancer treatment. PTT helps in precisely controlling the localized heat as well as deepening the therapeutic potential to tumor region [4]. Till date, various nanosized organic and inorganic photothermal agents, e.g. nanorods [5,6], photothermal dyes [7–9], nanoshells [10–14], etc. have been synthesized for the localized cancer therapy. However, uncontrolled particle size, low water dispersibility, poor biocompatibility, slow degradation, low therapeutic efficiency and poor light to heat conversion efficiency have been noticed and questioned. Although, Liu et al. reported some intelligent inorganic nanoparticles addressing several problems of tumor microenvironment towards radiation therapy for cancer [15–17].

Towards addressing these problems, earlier we have also shown the application of organo-inorganic nanomaterials as photothermal agents that include gold-coated liposomes (LiposAu) and poly(D, L-lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoshells (AuPLGA NS) that are biodegradable as well as suffice for the complete tumor ablation using standalone localized PTT [13,18]. Even though these nanohybrids proved to be promising photothermal agent, still the detainment of the photothermal agents at the tumor location for multiple shots of therapy and simultaneous release of anti-inflammatory drugs during laser irradiation are desirable.

On the other side, various forms of graphene (sheets to quantum dots) have been used for diverse biomedical applications [19–21]. Graphene oxide (GO), an oxidized derivative of graphene equipped with specific interfacial design (surface functional groups), is well-explored material for biomedical applications due to its high water dispersibility and easy entrapment of drugs and dyes [22–24]. Nanosized GO is highly active for bio-imaging and photothermal therapy due to quantum confinement and large ᴫ conjugation. Several anticancer drugs and dyes have been loaded with graphene oxide for the treatment of cancer [25]. The application of NIR dyes for the photothermal therapy has been moderated due to bleaching at the time of laser exposure and leaching from the carrier that unwantedly damages the nearby healthy tissue [26,27]. Although, bleaching problem of photothermal dye is being addressed by loading it with various carriers, still leaching of dye to nearby healthy tissue imposes big concern over cancer treatment [7]. Recently, it has been shown that loading of IR780 over graphene quantum dots increases the bioavailability, photostability and photothermal efficacy of IR780 and together makes an excellent photothermal agent [28]. On the other hand, nanoshells have emerged as a very effective photothermal agent because it is free from the effects of leaching and bleaching. Besides, it is facilitated with the tuning of plasmon resonance for treatment of a tumor residing at various depth and also allows clearance from the body if the degradable core is used [13,29,30]. It is also being used in several other biomedical applications like immunoassay, biosensing, etc. [31,32]. The heating potential of nanoshells has also been harnessed in the triggered release of cargo as well as activation of catalyst on laser irradiation [33,34].

Herein, we have designed a novel plasmonic carbon-based theranostic nanoplatform wherein, biodegradable AuPLGA NS were decorated over GO sheets, and its physical characteristics, as well as therapeutic efficacy in comparison to IR780 dye loaded GO (GO-IR780) has been comprehensively understood (Fig. 1 ).

Poly (DL-lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA; L/G of 50:50 and MW of 17,000) was gifted by PURAC Biomaterials, Netherlands. Graphite, d-α-tocopheryl poly (ethylene glycol 1000) succinate (TPGS), glycol chitosan (GCHT; ≥60% titration), polyethyleneimine (PEI, 50% w/v), 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), 2′,7′–dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA) and propidium iodide (PI) were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was procured from Spectrochem, India. Potassium carbonate (K2CO3), phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sulphuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Merck, India. Tetrakis hydroxymethyl phosphonium chloride (THPC 80%) was provided by Acros Organics, Belgium. Hydroxylamine hydrochloride was obtained from Fisher Scientific, USA. Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin-EDTA, Dulbecco’s phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) 1X w/o phenol red, calcium and magnesium, and antibiotic-antimycotic solution were procured from HiMedia Laboratories Ltd., India.

GO was synthesized from graphite powder by modified Hummers’ method [35]. Briefly, 500 mg of graphite (<20 μm, synthetic) and 250 mg of NaNO3 were added in 50 mL round-bottom flask with constant stirring maintained at 0 °C. Then, 12.5 mL of H2SO4 was added slowly with maintaining the temperature below 10 °C. After stirring the mixture for 40 min., 75 mg of KMnO4 was added slowly and stirred the mixture for another 30 min. keeping the temperature constant. 2 g of KMnO4 was again added slowly to the mixture for 45 min., and the temperature was maintained below 20 °C. The temperature of the reaction mixture was raised to 35 °C and allowed it to stir for 2 h. Thereafter, 25 mL of Milli-Q water was added to the reaction mixture and refluxed it at 90 °C for 15 min. The mixture was cooled down to room temperature and then 2 mL of 30% H2O2 was added to it. The color of resulting suspension was turned dark brown to bright yellow. 15 mL of Milli-Q water was added to the suspension and thereafter it was filtered under vacuum. The yellow cake was washed thrice with the 3% HCl and Milli-Q water to remove the residual metal ions and adjusted the pH to neutral. The as synthesized GO was dried over phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5) in a vacuum desiccator for 24 h and analysed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR; 3000 Hyperion Microscope, Bruker) and laser Raman spectroscopy (LRS; DILOR Z24). Morphology and surface of the GO were examined under field emission gun-scanning electron microscope (FEG-SEM, JEOL) and field emission gun-transmission electron microscope (FEG-TEM, JEOL).

GO was dispersed in Milli-Q water at a concentration of 1 mg/mL. It was bath sonicated for 3 h, probe sonicated for 1.5 h to produce a homogenous dispersion of exfoliated GO sheets. The morphology and thickness of the GO sheets were analysed under tapping mode of atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker). For sample preparation, few microliters of the diluted GO solution were drop-casted over thin mica sheets and air-dried. The GO sheets were imaged under FEG-TEM over lacey carbon grids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Poly (DL-lactic-co-glycolic acid) nanoparticles (PLGA NPs)
ce:section.ce:para: PLGA NPs were prepared by oil-in-water [O/W] single solvent evaporation method [18,36]. 25 mg PLGA was added to 10 mL of acetonitrile and kept overnight for complete dissolution. Next day, the organic phase containing PLGA polymer was added to 20 mL of water phase consisting of 0.025% d-α-tocopheryl poly (ethylene glycol 1000) succinate (TPGS) and 5 mg of glycol chitosan under vigorous stirring (1500 rpm). The emulsion was left overnight on stirring for the solvent evaporation at room temperature. Nanoparticles were pelleted down at 15,000 rpm and washed with Milli-Q water thrice. PLGA NPs were characterized using dynamic light scattering (DLS, Brookhaven), zeta potentiometer (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven), FTIR and FEG-SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of gold deposited plasmonic PLGA nanoshells (AuPLGA NS)
ce:section.ce:para: Gold seeds having a size of 3–4 nm were prepared using a method reported by Pham et al. [37]. The as prepared 5 mL of gold seeds were mixed with 500 μL of 1 mg/mL of PLGA NPs under vigorous stirring and subsequently incubated in the dark for 24 h. Concurrently, gold hydroxide was also prepared following a method reported by Duff et al. [38]. After 24 h of aging, 1 mL of gold seeds attached PLGA NPs were added to 40 mL of gold hydroxide and left to stir for 5 min. 5 mL of hydroxylammonium chloride was then added dropwise to grow gold seeds and form a shell over PLGA NPs. AuPLGA NS were pelleted down at 7000 rpm and characterized using UV–vis-NIR spectroscopy (Lambda 25, Perkin Elmer), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX, Oxford Instruments) and FEG-TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuPLGA NS decorated GO sheets (GO-AuPLGA)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 mg/mL of polyethyleneimine (PEI) was added dropwise to 0.5 mg/mL of GO in different volume ratio e.g. 1:0.1, 1:0.25, 1:0.75, 1:1, 1:3 and 1:5. The mixture was bath sonicated for 30 min. and thereafter stirred for 90 min. Excess PEI was removed by washing thrice with Milli-Q water. Thereafter, 6 mL of 75 μg/mL (determined by ICP-AES) of AuPLGA NS having the absorbance at ∼808 nm was added to 3 mL of GO-PEI and left for overnight stirring. The as-prepared nanohybrid GO-AuPLGA was characterized using UV–vis-NIR spectroscopy, FEG-TEM, and EDX line scan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of IR780 loaded GO sheets (GO-IR780)
ce:section.ce:para:
5 mL of 0.5 mg/mL GO was bath sonicated for 15 min., and thereafter different volume of 1 mM IR780 dye was added under vigorous stirring. The mixture was left on stirring in dark condition for 24 h. It was washed thrice to remove excess dye before dispersing in water. IR780 loading on GO was confirmed using UV–vis-NIR spectroscopy. The entrapment efficiency was calculated using the following formula.
%
E n t r a p m e n t
e f f i c i e n c y =
W
( A )
W
( B )
Where W(A) is the weight of the IR780 entrapped in GO-IR780 and W(B) is the weight of IR780 added.

To determine the stability of nanocomposites, 100 μl of sterile GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 were dispersed in 900 μl of PBS and 10% FBS. The solutions were kept at 37 °C under incubation for a week, thereafter DLS and Zeta analysis were performed.

Biocompatibility of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA was determined using MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay. The mouse normal fibroblast L929 cells, procured from National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS, Pune) were seeded at a density of ∼10^4 cells per well in 96-well plate and left for overnight incubation at 5% CO2 and 37 °C. 100 μL of different concentrations of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA were added and left for 24 h incubation. After 24 h, wells were washed off with phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) and 100 μL of 10% MTT dye in media was added to each well and kept for another 4 h. 200 μL of DMSO was added to each well to dissolve MTT crystals, and calorimetric readings were recorded at 570 and 690 nm using plate reader (Tecan Infinite Pro M 200).

To determine the heat generating potential of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA, 100 μL of test samples and controls were added in 96-well plate maintained at 37 °C and irradiated with 808 nm laser (CW, 500 mW). Samples were kept in widely separated wells to diminish the heat transfer. The laser was irradiated up to 10 min. and the increment in temperature was recorded using a digital probe thermometer at different time intervals.

The photothermal efficiency of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA was also determined using the following equation.
ƞ =
h S
T
M a x
-
T
S u r r
-
Q
D i s
I
1 -
10
- A 808
where ƞ is photothermal efficiency, h is a heat transfer coefficient, S is the surface area of the sample well, TMax is the maximum temperature attained at steady state by GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA, TSurr is the room temperature, QDis is the baseline energy or energy generated by the solvent, I is laser power, A808 is the absorbance of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA. The detailed calculation is provided in the supplementary information.

Cellular uptake of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA were examined qualitatively in human breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7) using confocal microscopy and quantitatively using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) and ICP-AES techniques. The cells of both cell lines were passaged and seeded with the cell number of 2.2×10^4 in each well of 12-well plates wherein each well was equipped with acid-etched glass coverslip and allowed to grow for 24 h at aforementioned incubation conditions. Then, the cells were treated with GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA at a concentration of 100 μg/mL concentration, the wells without nanomaterial were considered as negative control. The cells treated with GO-AuPLGA were washed with PBS, and the content of Au in the cells was analysed through ICP-AES for the quantitative uptake analysis. To study the quantitative uptake of GO-IR780 in MDA-MB-231 and MCF-7 cells, cells were seeded in two separate 6-well plates. After incubation for 24 h, the cells were treated with GO-IR780 at a concentration of 100 μg/mL for 24 h. The cells treated with IR780 at a concentration of 10 μM for 30 min. were considered as positive control. After treatment and subsequent incubation, cells were washed with PBS and trypsinized. The cells were again dispersed in PBS and centrifuged again to remove the traces of media. The pellet of cells were finally dispersed in 500 μL PBS with concomitant vortexing. The quantitative analysis of cellular uptake of GO-IR780 was done using FACS, the cellular uptake of GO-IR780 was compared with positive and negative control cells.

For the qualitative analysis of GO-IR780 uptake, the cells seeded over coverslips in the wells of two separate 12-well plates and treated with GO-IR780 for 24 h were washed with PBS and fixed with 3.7 % formaldehyde solution followed by washing with PBS thrice. The coverslips were mounted over the cleaned and disinfected glass slides and observed under Confocal Laser Scanning Microscope (CLSM, Olympus, Flow view) at 633 nm laser excitation. The cells were imaged under z-stack mode and further processed using ZEN (blue edition) software to analyse the localization of the GO-IR780.

Photothermal experiment was performed over MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells procured from NCCS, Pune. MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells were seeded in 96-well plate at a density of ∼5 × 10^4 and kept overnight in a CO2 incubator maintained at 37 °C. Next day, culture media was replaced with media containing different concentrations of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA. The wells were irradiated with 808 nm laser for 4 min., and the area of irradiation was kept same for both GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA. The plate was kept back in the incubator and after 24 h, MTT assay was performed as described earlier.

The possibility of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation was determined with the help of 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA) dye. The 100 μl of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA were added to MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells seeded in 96-well plate, and the following groups were formed- a) only GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA with 24 h incubation, b) GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA + NIR light irradiation (4 min.) + 24 h incubation, c) GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA + NIR light (4 min.) + 10 min. incubation. The H2O2 was used as positive control, and fluorescence was recorded at 520 nm using Tecan Infinite Pro M 200 plate reader.

Graphite was oxidized using the modified Hummers' method. Graphite oxide (GO) was characterized by FTIR and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to establish the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (ESI & Fig. 2 e, g–i) [34,39,40]. FTIR and Raman spectra (defects in the structure) of GO confirm the oxidation of graphite (ESI & Fig. 2f).

The GO was exfoliated using bath sonicator and probe sonicator. The exfoliated GO was further characterized using AFM, FEG-TEM, and FEG-SEM. It was observed that GO sheets were well exfoliated to few layered thick graphene oxide and swiftly dispersed in the water. The AFM image and its section analysis shows that sheets were nanometers in size and around ∼1.4 nm in thickness (Fig. 2a). The electron microscopy micrographs confirmed the exfoliation of the GO sheets (Fig. 2b and c). A typical hexagonal pattern was observed in selected area electron diffraction (SAED) ( Fig. 2d). The as-synthesized GO was found to be negatively charged with a zeta potential of −39.19 ± 1.43 mV (Fig. S4).

PLGA nanoparticles were prepared by a solvent evaporation method using glycol chitosan which is an antibacterial and antifungal agent as well as with P-glycoprotein (P-gp) inhibitor substance like TPGS [41,42]. The average hydrodynamic diameter determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS) was found to be ∼100 nm and size observed in FEG-SEM was ∼ 80 nm (Fig. S1). Zeta potential was found to be around −25 ± 2.36 mV.

Gold coating over PLGA nanoparticles was performed via ex situ method [43]. Gold seeds synthesized using tetrakis (hydroxymethyl) phosphonium chloride (THPC) were found to retain the size of ∼ 3–4 nm and well dispersed (Fig. S2). To attach negatively charged (−18 ± 1.32 mV) gold seeds, PLGA nanoparticles were functionalized with positively charged biocompatible polymer i.e. glycol chitosan. Glycol chitosan was preferred over chitosan due to its solubility in water at physiological pH and remarkable reflection of enhanced permeability and retention effect [44]. The coating of glycol chitosan over PLGA nanoparticles was confirmed with the change in zeta potential from −25 ± 2.36 mV to + 30 ± 1.74 mV. The meticulous removal of excess gold seeds is necessary else they grow into big gold nanoparticles during shell formation, and a prominent peak around 550 nm is observed [37,45]. After addition of gold hydroxide, gold seeds attached over PLGA NPs were grown into big gold nanoparticles and formed the shell. The integrity of shell and establishment of gold were confirmed using FEG-TEM and EDX ( Fig. 3 a, b (inset)). Vitamin E modified gold coated PLGA nanoshells (AuPLGA NS) were observed to be uniform and intact with zeta potential −31.82 mV ± 1.62 mV indicating excellent stability (Fig. S4). Absorbance peak of AuPLGA NS could be tailored at any wavelength in the NIR region by varying the amount of gold hydroxide. The plasmon resonance of nanoshells is reported to be dependent over core-shell diameter ratio [43]. In the present case, the plasmon resonance of AuPLGA NS was tailored at 808 nm for performing the comparative study with IR780.

GO surface was functionalized with cationic polymer polyethyleneimine (PEI) to attach negatively charged AuPLGA NS. It was observed that 1:5 volumetric ratio of GO and PEI resulted in stable dispersion while other lower ratios (1:3, 1:1, 1:0.75, 1:0.25, 1:0.1) showed aggregation (Fig. S3), the stability of the solution of GO-PEI (1:5) occurs due to repulsion between positively charged GO-PEI sheets. At lower volume ratio, PEI didn’t coat GO completely and led to aggregation due to electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged GO and PEI whereas at higher volume ratio PEI completely coated the GO and colloidal stability obtained due to electrostatic repulsion between similarly charged moieties [46,47]. After PEI coating, the zeta potential of GO changed from -39.19 ± 1.43 mV to +29.75 ± 2.55 mV affirmed the successful coating of PEI (Fig. S4). It was found that there was no significant change in the structure and morphology of GO after PEI coating. The SAED pattern was found simple with dull brightness and to be masked due to the layering of PEI over GO surface (Fig. 3c & f).

The negatively charged AuPLGA NS were attached to positively charged PEI coated GO via electrostatic attraction. It was observed that AuPLGA NS were well decorated over GO sheets (Fig. 3d & e). The UV–vis-NIR absorbance of the AuPLGA NS attached graphene oxide sheets (GO-AuPLGA) showed overlapping with the parent GO absorbance peak, and a scattered pattern of dots with a hexagon pattern in SAED confirm the attachment of nanoshells over GO sheets (Fig. 3g & h). Further, EDX line scan explained the profile of different elements, where carbon and nitrogen found to be uniformly distributed while gold dispersed discontinuously. It further reinforces the ex-situ synthesis of AuPLGA NS and its decoration over the GO (Fig. 4a & b).

IR780 dye is a well-studied photothermal agent and has been used in combination with several nanostructures for photothermal treatment and near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) imaging [48–50,7,51]. IR780 dye was loaded to negatively charged GO by incubating them together (Fig. 4 c). GO-IR780 was found to show differential contrast and dark patches over the surface. A staggered pattern of dots consisting of multiple hexagons in SAED indicate that the IR780 might have attached over GO sheets by both sides non covalently hence ended up with such diffraction pattern ( Fig. 4f). Dye entrapment efficiency was determined to be ∼30% which is quite higher than the previous report [28]. Different concentrations of IR780 and GO were used to obtain the maximum loading efficiency. The loading relationship was found to be linear up to 0.2 mg of dye. IR780 shows a prominent peak at around 780 nm, however, after loading on GO, remarkable changes were observed in its native peak. The absorption peak at 238 nm is assigned to the π-π * transition of CC in sp2 aromatic domains. The peak at 297 nm is assigned to the n-π * transition of CO bonds. UV–vis-NIR absorbance of GO-IR780 showed red shift in the peak positions which their individual counter parts show (Fig. 4d, e & g), that supposedly has occurred due to π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction between GO and IR780 [52]. However, the fluorescence emission peak of IR780 is blue-shifted after attachment over the surface of GO with reduced intensity (Fig. S5).

The stability of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 was also determined in different dispersing media (e.g. PBS and 10% FBS) for a week. In water, the average hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 was determined to be ∼391 nm, +16.03 mV and ∼350 nm, −24.733 mV, respectively. After a week-long dispersion in PBS, the hydrodynamic size of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 increased to ∼3750 nm and ∼3794 nm, respectively; and zeta potential reduced to +1.822 and −7.75 mV, respectively whereas in 10% FBS, the hydrodynamic size of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 changed to ∼1210 nm and ∼700.5 nm, respectively and zeta potential of GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 changed to −10.19 and −7.66 mV, respectively (Supplementary Information, Table 1 & S4). Although, there was a change in stability of the nanohybrids in a week, the increase in size might further reinforce the retention of GO-AuLGA and GO-IR780 for repetitive and highly localized photothermal therapy after intratumoral administration.

The GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 are uptaken by the MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cancer cells efficiently, however, some of the patches are also found attached over the surface of the cells that remained even after washing with PBS (Fig. S8–10). The GO-IR780 showed a competitive cellular uptake as compare to bare IR780, it showed 93.8 % in MCF-7 cells and 89.1 % in MDA-MB-231 cells whereas the values for IR780 were found to be 99.5 % and 99.3 %, respectively (Fig. S7). The cellular uptake of GO-AuPLGA was studied in MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells through ICP-AES, the values were found to be 72.9 % and 67.3 %, respectively (Fig. S6). The attachment of GO with IR780 reduces fluorescence of the dye but does not quench it completely hence that helped to locate the GO-IR780 in both cancer cell lines (Fig. S8–10) [52–54]. The localization of GO-IR780 in both cell lines was examined using confocal microscopy (Fig. S8) and analysed further through 2.5D, 3D, and orthogonal imaging (Fig. S9–10).

The biocompatibility of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA was determined quantitatively as well as qualitatively using MTT assay over mouse normal fibroblasts (L929) cells. The GO-AuPLGA didn’t show any toxicity and %cell viability was similar to negative control (NC, cells without any treatment) even at the double therapeutic dosage while GO-IR780 found to be little toxic at the therapeutic and double the therapeutic dosage. The morphology of L929 cells also found to be intact and spindle-shaped for GO-AuPLGA (Fig. 5a & b). It has been found that IR780 dye preferentially accumulates in mitochondria and cause the depolarization of the membrane as well as generates the reactive oxygen species which leads to apoptosis [55]. The density of formazan crystals was also found to be similar in GO-AuPLGA and NC (Fig. S11).

In comparative phototransduction study, it was observed that IR780 dye bleached out as the temperature reached to ∼42.4 °C while in the case of GO-IR780, temperature reached to ∼45.2 °C in 2 min. of laser irradiation, after that it became constant at around ∼44.5 °C. In the case of GO-AuPLGA, the temperature increment was up to 43 °C within 2 min. of laser irradiation, and afterward, it became constant at around 44 °C. In controls, i.e. water and GO, the temperature didn’t exceed above 39 °C and 41.5 °C, respectively ( Fig. 5 c). The change in color of photothermal agents after laser irradiation also reflects their stability during the photothermal experiment as the IR780 dye was bleached after 4 min. of laser exposure while GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA didn’t show any significant change in color (Fig. S12). The photothermal efficiency of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA was also determined, and it was found to be 22.75% and 22.01%, respectively. The detailed calculation is provided in the supplementary information and Fig. S13.

The photothermal ablation potential of GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA was checked on the breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231). In quantitative analysis, it was found that at the sub-therapeutic dosage, both GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA reduced the viability of MCF-7 cells up to ∼ 30% when 808 nm (CW, 500 mW) laser was irradiated for 4 min. However, in the case of MDA-MB-231, the cell death obtained was only up to ∼ 35% for GO-IR780 and 45% for GO-AuPLGA. At therapeutic conc., both GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA reduced the viability up to ∼13% in MCF-7. While, in MDA-MB-231, GO-AuPLGA reduced cell viability only up to ∼35%. At double the therapeutic dosage, in MCF-7 cells, there was no significant change in % cell death in comparison to therapeutic dosage was observed, which confirms the already complete ablation of cancer cells by GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA at therapeutic dosage. In MDA-MB-231, the % cell death in double the therapeutic dosage remained similar to therapeutic dosage in case of GO-IR780 while it increased up to ∼15.9% in case of GO-AuPLGA. GO-IR780 was found to be little toxic (65% for MDA-MB-231) even in the absence of laser while GO-AuPLGA didn’t show any significant toxicity even at the double therapeutic dosage for either cell line ( Fig. 5d). The quantitative result was also reflected in the qualitative analysis performed with the help of PI (Fig. 6 a & b). It was observed that number of dead cells (red spots) were very less in the group ‘Cells’, ‘Cells + Laser’, ‘Cells + GO-AuPLGA’ in comparison to ‘Cells + GO-IR780′ and photothermal treatment groups (‘Cells + GO-IR780 + Laser’; ‘Cells + GO-AuPLGA + Laser’). The morphology of the cells incubated with GO-IR780 also changed to circular due to dose-related toxicity of IR780. However, the cells incubated with GO-AuPLGA remained well-spread and spindle-shaped.

The possibility of reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation both with and without laser irradiation was also determined. It was found that GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA didn’t generate ROS significantly even after 24 h of incubation with MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 cells. However, when the laser was irradiated for 4 min. and thereafter cells were incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. ROS were generated. The ROS signal after 24 h of laser irradiation was also checked, however during the washing of excess dyes, detached dead cells washed off along with the GO-IR780 and GO-AuPLGA resulting in reduced ROS signal apparently (Fig. S14).

In reference to the above findings, it could be envisaged that nanoshells embedded graphene oxide delivered the photothermal therapy in highly localized fashion and without any toxicity to healthy cells. Although GO-IR780 also acts as an efficient photothermal agent, it’s leaching to healthy tissue and dose-dependent toxicities are questioning the localized therapeutic efficacy of photothermal therapy.

It was found that both GO-AuPLGA and GO-IR780 are the promising photothermal agent. However, leaching of IR780 to nearby healthy cells undesirably show toxic effect without any laser irradiation. Also, bleaching of IR780 on laser irradiation limits the usage to only one shot of therapy. In this regard, it would be more beneficial if instead of loading anti-cancer drug/dye to nanohybrid, anti-inflammatory or anaesthetic drugs are used in combination with efficacious NIR light responsive plasmonic biodegradable nanostructures towards localized treatment via photothermal therapy and reducing the systemic toxicity.
There are no conflicts of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to PURAC, Netherlands for providing PLGA as gift sample. Also, thankful to IIT Bombay Healthcare, SAIF and IRCC for providing financial support and instrument facility. We also thank Mr. Ramkrishn Gupta from NanoBios Lab for fruitful discussion.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.08.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
